nativeRange.habitat: The Annularis Angelfish inhabits coastal rocky coral reefs, reef caves, and wrecks at depths of 3 to 30 metres. Juveniles tend to occupy shallow inshore areas with algae-covered substrates.

diet.notes: Feeds on sponges, tunicates, filamentous algae, and zooplankton in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts angelfish preparations containing sponge matter, frozen meaty foods (chopped seafood, mysis), and marine algae/nori. Newly acquired specimens may initially prefer live or fresh foods such as brine shrimp or chopped seafood before transitioning to prepared diets.

imageCandidates.notes: Adult specimen photographed by Gerald R. Allen. Thumbnail form at /images/thumbnails/jpg/tn_Poann_ud.jpg.

summary: A large Indo-Pacific angelfish recognized by its brilliant sapphire-blue ring markings and curved horizontal lines over a golden-brown body. Generally considered one of the more demanding large Pomacanthus species, best suited for experienced keepers with a spacious, well-established system.
careNotes: The Annularis Angelfish (Pomacanthus annularis) is a large marine angelfish native to coastal reefs of the Indo-West Pacific, ranging from East Africa through Indonesia and New Guinea to New Caledonia, and north to southern Japan. Adults are documented reaching up to 45 cm total length in the wild, though aquarium specimens are commonly traded at 20–23 cm. The species tends to be semi-aggressive and may become territorial once established; tankmates should be of similar or larger size and similarly assertive temperament. It is generally not considered reef-safe: it has been known to nip at sessile invertebrates including corals, nudibranchs, and tridacnid clams and is best kept in a fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system. This species is a sequential hermaphrodite (protogynous); males and females cannot be visually distinguished as adults. Juveniles undergo a dramatic multi-year color transformation so complete that they are sometimes not recognized as the same species: juveniles are dark with brilliant white and electric-blue vertical stripes, while adults display bold sapphire-blue ring markings and curved horizontal lines over a golden-brown body. Aquarists should be aware the fish purchased as a juvenile will look entirely different at maturity. The Annularis is documented as susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), which tends to develop in poor water quality or when the diet lacks sufficient marine algae and vitamin-rich foods; a varied diet and pristine water parameters are generally advised to prevent this. Adults have been observed in pairs inside reef caves in the wild.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Annularis Angelfish has not been documented, and captive breeding of large Pomacanthus angelfish species is rarely achieved. In the wild, adults have been reported in pairs on coastal reef systems. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning the dominant individual in a social group transitions from female to male over time.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adultSizeCm (45 cm TL), temperature (26.3–29.1°C), native range, taxonomy, and diet. Temperature disagreement with LiveAquaria (72–78°F / 22–26°C vs FishBase 26–29°C); averaged to 24–27.5°C for the final record.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Pomacanthus annularis
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (45 cm / 18 in), native range (East Africa to Japan), taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), and ontogenetic color change. Used for taxonomy and range cross-verification only.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite in which the dominant individual transitions from female to male. Adults have occasionally been observed in pairs in reef caves in the wild.
